onScreenText: What happens when you click a link?
The answer involves more steps than you think
scenes.narration: A single click fires off an invisible chain reaction across the internet.

onScreenText: See a full HTTP request cycle
Watch the browser render a page
scenes.narration: In this video, we'll trace a web request from URL bar to rendered page, step by step.
First stop, the internet's massive phone book.

onScreenText: Computers use IP addresses like 142.250.80.46 to find each other. DNS translates the domain name you type into the IP address the network needs.
It's like looking up a contact name in your phone to find their number.
scenes.narration: Your browser has no idea where google.com lives. It asks DNS for the actual address every time.
Browser cache misses first, then it climbs up the chain. OS cache, router, ISP resolver, and only then the root servers.

onScreenText: DNS is a massive, distributed phone book. You give it a name, it gives you the number. And just like a phone book, it gets cached so you don't look up the same name twice.
scenes.narration: Hand it a name, get a number back, cached so you never ask twice.

onScreenText: DNS converts human-readable domain names into machine-readable IP addresses. Every web request starts here.
scenes.narration: Every request you'll ever make on the web starts right here, with DNS.
Now let's talk about opening the connection.

onScreenText: Before any data moves, your browser and the server need to agree they can talk. TCP guarantees that every packet arrives, in order, without corruption.
Imagine calling someone. You don't just start talking, you wait for them to pick up first.
scenes.narration: TCP makes both sides agree they're ready before a single byte moves. No handshake, no data.

onScreenText: Client says: I want to connect
Server says: Got it, I'm ready
scenes.narration: Three quick steps and you're in. Client sends SYN, server replies SYN-ACK, client confirms with an ACK.

onScreenText: You dial, they pick up and say hello, you confirm you hear them. Three steps before you say a single useful word. TCP works the same way.
scenes.narration: You dial, they answer, you confirm. Three steps before one useful word gets through.

onScreenText: TCP's three-way handshake ensures both sides are ready before any data is sent. It's slower, but nothing gets lost.
scenes.narration: It's slower than blasting raw packets, but nothing gets lost or arrives scrambled.
Connection's open, now the actual conversation starts.

onScreenText: Your browser sends a request with a method, URL, and headers. The server reads it, does its work, and sends back a response with a status code and data.
It's ordering at a restaurant. You say what you want, the kitchen makes it, and they bring it back.
scenes.narration: Think of it like a restaurant. Browser places an order, kitchen fills it, response comes back.
GET reads data from the server, POST sends data to it. Same protocol, completely different jobs.

onScreenText:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html
Content-Length: 1256
Cache-Control: max-age=3600

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
  <body>Hello World</body>
</html>
scenes.narration: That two hundred OK means everything worked. Content type says it's HTML, and cache control says keep this for an hour.
Over five billion of these requests fire off every single day worldwide.

onScreenText: HTTP is the language browsers and servers speak. Every web interaction is a request-response pair.
scenes.narration: One request, one response. The whole web runs on this dead simple pattern.
Now let's peek inside the server itself.
Request hits the router, which matches it to a handler. Handler runs your logic, queries the database, then sends back a response.
Static pages are pre-built, identical for everyone. Dynamic ones get generated fresh each request, personalized for you.

onScreenText: Users can modify anything in the browser. Always validate inputs on the server. Client checks are for UX, server checks are for security.
scenes.narration: Users can fake anything in the browser. Always validate server-side, no exceptions ever.

onScreenText: The server routes requests, runs business logic, talks to the database, and sends back responses. It's the brain of every web app.
scenes.narration: Routing, logic, database, then response. The server's the brain of your web app.

onScreenText: From raw HTML to pixels on screen
scenes.narration: Last piece, how raw HTML becomes pixels.
HTML becomes the DOM, CSS becomes the CSSOM, they merge into a render tree, layout calculates positions, then paint draws pixels.

onScreenText: The browser can't show anything until it builds the DOM and CSSOM. That's why blocking CSS and JavaScript in the head slow down your page.
You can't hang a painting until the wall is built and painted. Same idea.
scenes.narration: Nothing renders until DOM and CSSOM are built. Blocking CSS or scripts in the head stalls everything.
Server processing dominates here by far. Download and render follow, while DNS and TCP stay surprisingly quick.

onScreenText: The browser parses HTML and CSS, builds a render tree, calculates layout, and paints pixels. This all happens in milliseconds.
scenes.narration: Raw HTML to pixels in milliseconds. Your browser is shockingly fast at this.

onScreenText: DNS resolves domain names to IPs
The server processes and queries data
The browser parses, lays out, and paints
scenes.narration: DNS finds the address, TCP opens the line, HTTP carries the conversation, server does the work, and your browser paints the result.
If this helped, hit subscribe and I'll catch you in the next one.
